title: Kinetic kagome magnetism: from self-trapping RVB polarons to semiclassical correlations
authors: Yufei Pei, Shuai A. Chen, Claudio Castelnovo, Roderich Moessner
abstract: To gain deeper insight into the role of hole kinetics in determining magnetism in highly frustrated doped Mott insulators, we consider the single-hole counter-Nagaoka problem on the kagome lattice, using magnetization as a tuning parameter. Near full polarization, a doped hole delocalizes upon binding reversed spins in a pattern of singlet bonds which we term resonating-valence-bond (RVB) polaron. These RVB polarons can have extremely small effective bandwidths, and hence exhibit self-trapping. By tuning the spin polarization, we track the evolution of these states toward the unpolarized sector, where we observe the emergence of $\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}$ antiferromagnetic correlation reminiscent of the classical Potts and Heisenberg models on the kagome lattice. These results provide a framework to understand how RVB physics at short scales evolves into conventional magnetic correlations at long scales.
summary: This paper investigates kinetic magnetism on the kagome lattice by studying how a single hole interacts with localized spins, forming RVB polarons. The authors show that as the system moves toward an unpolarized state, these short-range quantum correlations evolve into robust, long-range $\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}$ antiferromagnetic order. By combining ED and DMRG, they bridge the gap between localized valence bond physics and conventional magnetic ordering.
main_problem: The study investigates how hole kinetics dictate magnetism in highly frustrated doped Mott insulators, specifically tracking the transition from short-range RVB polaron physics to long-range magnetic correlations.
main_result: The research demonstrates a conceptual bridge where localized RVB polarons at low spin polarization evolve into conventional $\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}$ antiferromagnetic order in the unpolarized limit.
method: The authors employ Exact Diagonalization (ED) and Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) techniques, supplemented by variational calculations using Husimi cactus clusters to model the hole-spin interactions.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as a single-hole counter-Nagaoka problem on the kagome lattice, governed by an infinite-$U$ Hubbard Hamiltonian. The physics is analyzed by tuning the magnetization parameter (doping level $n$).
key_observables: Polaron bandwidth, spin and hole density profiles, spin structure factor $S(\mathbf{k})$, and ground state energy scaling.
important_parameters: Magnetization/spin polarization ($n$), lattice geometry (kagome), and the transition between small-$n$ (localized) and large-$n$ (semiclassical) regimes.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on modeling polarons using Husimi cactus clusters, and extrapolations to infinite systems from finite-size DMRG/ED calculations are necessary but carry potential self-interaction errors.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the formation of RVB polarons (Fig. 3), show band structures and spin correlations for small $n$ (Fig. 2, Fig. S1), and compare the resulting $\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}$ magnetic order to classical Potts model benchmarks (Fig. S5, Fig. S12).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the polaron physics via ED for small doping ($n=1$ to $5$), showing self-trapping; it then uses DMRG for larger systems ($n\geq 5$) to observe the recovery of delocalized behavior and $\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}$ order in the unpolarized limit, benchmarking this against classical models.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical mechanism linking microscopic quantum entanglement (RVB singlets) to macroscopic magnetic ordering ($\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3}$), which is crucial for understanding high-temperature superconductivity and frustrated magnetism in correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Kinetic magnetism in the presence of frustration favors the formation of valence bond singlets. (b) Kagome lattice geometry used in our simulations, for system size 𝑁= 3𝐿𝑥𝐿𝑦. We also show an example of a compact localized state around a single plaquette [24].

FIG. 2. Band structure for the 1H1S system of size 12 × 12 (a), ob- tained using ED; the dashed line shows a single-particle tight-binding fit with 𝑡eﬀ≃−4.2 × 10−3 and offset ≃2.67. Spin density (b) and spin correlations (c) (red ferro and blue antiferro, with width propor- tional to their strength), for fixed hole position. Spin density pro- file (d) along the horizontal line in panel (b), for 1H𝑛S systems with 𝑛= 1, . . . , 4, comparing ED (solid squares) with Husimi cactus clus- ter results (open squares). The dependence of the GS energy of the 1H𝑛S systems on 𝑛is shown in panel (e), with the inset plotting our best estimate for the width of the lowest band from ED (see SI).

FIG. 3. Examples of Husimi cactus clusters used in the main text to understand the nature of the polarons. All symmetry-inequivalent clusters are shown for 1H𝑛S, with 1 ≤𝑛≤4. For 𝑛= 4, there are three possible configurations: a star-shaped one shown in the main figure, and two linear ones shown in the inset. For 𝑛= 5, only the most relevant shape is shown; with its five symmetry-equivalent part- ners, this shape covers a 12-site (shaded) region of the lattice. For each cluster, only one of the possible hole positions, and its corre- sponding valence bond arrangement, are shown.

FIG. 4. Reversed spin (a) and hole (b) densities for one of the seemingly-localized 1H5S GSs. Profile cuts are shown in panel (c). The hole density is well-described by a simple model of a spinless fermion (see inset and main text) with unfrustrated hopping on a 12- site cluster (𝑡= −1, yellow edges), embedded on a periodic kagome lattice with frustrated hopping (𝑡= 1, black edges). The spin correla- tors (red for ferro, blue for antiferro) are shown in panel (d), with the thickness of the lines denoting their strength. Density results were obtained using DMRG and spin correlators using ED (see EM). A vanishing pinning potential was used to single out a given location for the RVB polaron in...

FIG. 5. Rescaled peak intensity at the 𝐾point for our unpolarized system on lattices of size 𝐿𝑥× 3, with 6 ≤𝐿𝑥≤24, obtained using DMRG (circles joined by solid lines). We also show the 3 × 3 result obtained using ED (yellow triangle). We compare these results with the behavior of the 3-state Potts model on lattices of size 𝐿𝑥×3, with 6 ≤𝐿𝑥≤24 (squares and dotted lines), which in 𝑑= 2 is known to sit on a KT transition between algebraic and long-range magnetic correlations.
